one_line_takeaway: A black-box MCTS-guided fuzzer that automatically evolves indirect-prompt-injection payloads, roughly doubling handcrafted-baseline success against GPT-4o/o3-mini LLM agents on AgentDojo and VWA-adv, though gains largely evaporate against Claude-3.5-Sonnet and under several defenses.
key_finding: See structured fields; headline: 71% ASR on AgentDojo (o3-mini, fuzzing set) and 60% on VWA-adv (GPT-4o, per tables), roughly doubling the ~38%/36% handcrafted baselines, with test-set generalization of 65%/59%.
core_contribution: AGENTVIGIL is presented by the authors as the first generic black-box fuzzing framework for automatically discovering and exploiting indirect prompt injection (IPI) vulnerabilities in LLM agents, without access to the agents' internal LLMs or architecture. Its own novel components (explicitly distinguished from cited work such as GPTFuzzer, AgentPoison, and VWA-adv, which the paper cites as prior/baseline) are: (1) a hand-curated high-quality initial corpus of adversarial prompt templates with placeholders (role-playing, delimiter-based, obfuscation strategies); (2) an adaptive seed-scoring strategy that combines attack success rate with a coverage bonus rewarding seeds that succeed on previously-failed tasks, giving denser feedback than the sparse binary success signal; (3) a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) seed-selection algorithm using UCB1 to balance exploitation/exploration over a mutation tree; and (4) five LLM-driven mutators (Shorten, Expand, Rephrase, Crossover, GenerateSimilar) run by a small helper LLM (GPT-4o-mini). The paper frames its novelty against GPTFuzzer, which does single-turn white/full-control direct prompt injection jailbreaks; AGENTVIGIL instead targets multi-step agents where the attacker can only influence external data and observes only binary success feedback.
threat_model: Black-box: neither users nor attackers see the underlying LLM internals or the agent architecture; only external system behavior is observable. The user is benign, issuing legitimate tasks. The attacker interacts with the agent as an ordinary user and can influence only external data sources (e.g., editing a shopping-site item, a customer review, or a calendar event) — i.e., indirect prompt injection, not control of the user prompt. The attacker observes only a binary success/failure signal per task (e.g., checking whether a malicious email reached a recipient) and aims to redirect the agent to attacker-chosen goals (visit phishing URLs, download malware, exfiltrate private data). Explicitly out of scope: misusing agents to perform harmful actions directly, and attacks on the hosting/computational infrastructure.
limitations: Attacks transfer poorly to Claude-3.5-Sonnet (as low as 0.03-0.04 on AgentDojo), which the authors attribute to Claude's robustness and a different vulnerability profile (they suspect it is more susceptible to simpler prompts). Defenses materially reduce ASR, and in multiple cells AGENTVIGIL falls below the handcrafted baseline (AgentDojo repeat 0.12<0.21; VWA-adv safety 0.29<0.34 and combined 0.27<0.30), so the paper's own numbers undercut a strong "robust against defenses" claim; the authors note complex prompts struggle under VWA-adv's limited context. There is a prose/abstract-vs-table inconsistency on the headline VWA-adv number (70% claimed vs 60% in Tables 1 and 3). Evaluation is confined to two benchmarks plus a single local (non-live, ethically constrained) WebArena case study; some defenses were excluded for compatibility/cost reasons. Gemini/DeepSeek/open-source models could not be fully evaluated on AgentDojo due to tool-call limitations.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (personal-assistant agent benchmark; 142-task fuzzing set / 173-task test set)
VWA-adv (web-agent benchmark built on VisualWebArena; 99-task fuzzing set / 100-task test set, text-trigger tasks)
WebArena shopping site (local magento2 deployment) for the real-world case study
quantitative_results: AgentDojo o3-mini fuzzing set: AGENTVIGIL 71% ASR vs handcrafted baseline 38%; initial corpus alone 63%
AgentDojo o3-mini test set: AGENTVIGIL 65% vs baseline 34%
VWA-adv GPT-4o: Tables 1 (fuzzing) and 3 (no-defense) report 60% vs baseline 36% (abstract/prose state 70% — internal discrepancy)
VWA-adv GPT-4o test set: AGENTVIGIL 59% vs baseline 44%
Transfer to unseen LLM Gemini-2-flash-exp (VWA-adv): 67% (test set) vs baseline 50%
Claude-3.5-Sonnet highly robust: AgentDojo 0.03 (fuzzing)/0.04 (test); VWA-adv 0.31 (fuzzing)/0.42 (test), sometimes below baseline
AgentDojo defenses (o3-mini, fuzzing) — AGENTVIGIL vs baseline: pi_detector 0.25 vs 0.13, repeat 0.12 vs 0.21, delimit 0.49 vs 0.36
VWA-adv defenses (GPT-4o) — AGENTVIGIL vs baseline: safety 0.29 vs 0.34, paraphrase 0.33 vs 0.27, combined 0.27 vs 0.30
Ablation: replacing the curated corpus with baseline prompts plateaus success after ~4 iterations; removing adaptive scoring + MCTS selection markedly slows improvement
baselines_compared: AgentDojo handcrafted adversarial prompts (Debenedetti et al., 2024)
VWA-adv handcrafted adversarial prompts (Wu et al., 2024b)
AGENTVIGIL's own initial high-quality corpus (as an internal reference point)
Ablated AGENTVIGIL variants (w/o initial corpus; w/o adaptive scoring + MCTS selection)
